title: HardInterventionOn

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_10 -- "HardInterventionOn".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_10
% title: HardInterventionOn
\def\rowref{def\_3\_10}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_10}

\begin{Def}[Hard intervention on CDMGs]\label{def:G_hard_intervention}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
  The \emph{intervened CDMG} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: 
  \[ G_{\doit(W)}:=(J_{\doit(W)},V_{\doit(W)},E_{\doit(W)},L_{\doit(W)}),\] 
  %\[G(V\sm W|\doit(W \cup J)):=G_{\doit(W)}:=(J_{\doit(W)},V_{\doit(W)},E_{\doit(W)},L_{\doit(W)}),\] 
  where:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\doit(W)}:= J \cup W$,
      \item $V_{\doit(W)}:= V \sm W$,
      \item $E_{\doit(W)}:= E \sm \lC v \tuh w \,|\, v \in G, w  \in W  \rC$,
      \item $L_{\doit(W)}:= L \sm \lC v \huh w\,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$,
  \end{enumerate}
  where we turn all nodes from $W$ into input nodes and remove all edges into nodes from $W$.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. The intervened CDMG w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: \[ G_{\doit(W)}:=(J_{\doit(W)},V_{\doit(W)},E_{\doit(W)},L_{\doit(W)}),\]

where:

  1. $J_{\doit(W)}:= J \cup W$,
  2. $V_{\doit(W)}:= V \sm W$,
  3. $E_{\doit(W)}:= E \sm \lC v \tuh w \,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$,
  4. $L_{\doit(W)}:= L \sm \lC v \huh w\,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$,

where we turn all nodes from $W$ into input nodes and remove all edges into nodes from $W$.

lean.name: hardInterventionOn
lean.code:
/-- The *hard intervention* of the CDMG `G` with respect to a set
of nodes `W ⊆ α`: the new CDMG `G_{do(W)}` obtained by promoting
every vertex of `W` to an input node, removing every directed
edge whose *target* lies in `W`, and removing every bidirected
edge incident to *any* vertex of `W`. See
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` definition
`def:G_hard_intervention` (def 3.10 of the LN).

This is intentionally well-defined for *every* `W : Set α`, with
no `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition -- see the design note above for
why (the iterated form `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁).hardInterventionOn
W₂` of claim_3_4 needs this generality). The four `@[simp]` lemmas
`hardInterventionOn_J`, `hardInterventionOn_V`,
`mem_hardInterventionOn_E`, `mem_hardInterventionOn_L` below
characterise the four components of the result. -/
def hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) : CDMG α where
  J := G.J ∪ W
  V := G.V \ W
  disjoint_JV := by
    rw [Set.disjoint_left]
    rintro x (hJ | hW) ⟨hV, hnW⟩
    · exact Set.disjoint_left.mp G.disjoint_JV hJ hV
    · exact hnW hW
  E := G.E \ { p : α × α | p.2 ∈ W }
  E_subset := by
    rintro ⟨v, w⟩ ⟨hE, hnW⟩
    obtain ⟨hv, hw⟩ := G.E_subset hE
    refine ⟨?_, hw, hnW⟩
    rcases hv with hJ | hV
    · exact Or.inl (Or.inl hJ)
    · by_cases hW : v ∈ W
      · exact Or.inl (Or.inr hW)
      · exact Or.inr ⟨hV, hW⟩
  L := G.L \ { p : α × α | p.1 ∈ W ∨ p.2 ∈ W }
  L_subset := by
    rintro ⟨v₁, v₂⟩ ⟨hL, hnW⟩
    obtain ⟨h1, h2⟩ := G.L_subset hL
    exact ⟨⟨h1, fun hW => hnW (Or.inl hW)⟩, ⟨h2, fun hW => hnW (Or.inr hW)⟩⟩
  L_irrefl := by
    intro v₁ v₂ h
    exact G.L_irrefl h.1
  L_symm := by
    intro v₁ v₂ h
    exact ⟨G.L_symm h.1, fun hh => h.2 hh.symm⟩
  disjoint_EL := by
    rw [Set.disjoint_left]
    rintro p ⟨hE, _⟩ ⟨hL, _⟩
    exact Set.disjoint_left.mp G.disjoint_EL hE hL

lean_explanation:
The definition takes a graph `G : CDMG α` (the type parameter `α` is the ambient carrier of nodes, matching the LN's silent assumption that the graph is over some node type) and a set `W : Set α` to intervene on, and produces a new `CDMG α`. The LN's $G_{\doit(W)}$ becomes `G.hardInterventionOn W` in Lean; the dot-projection reads as "the hard intervention of `G` on `W`".

The four data fields are direct transcriptions of the LN's four bullets: `J := G.J ∪ W` and `V := G.V \ W` are ordinary set union and set difference; `E := G.E \ { p | p.2 ∈ W }` removes directed edges whose *target* `p.2` lies in `W`; `L := G.L \ { p | p.1 ∈ W ∨ p.2 ∈ W }` removes bidirected edges with *either* endpoint in `W`. The disjunction in `L` is necessary because Lean encodes bidirected edges as ordered pairs in `Set (α × α)` together with a symmetry obligation `L_symm` (def_3_1); see the design note below.

Beyond the data, a `CDMG` carries six proof obligations -- `disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL` -- which together encode that $J$ and $V$ are disjoint, every edge has endpoints in $J \cup V$, $L$ is symmetric and irreflexive, and $E \cap L = \emptyset$. The definition discharges all six inline; each is a short consequence of the corresponding field of `G` combined with a set-membership manipulation against the new deletions. Four accompanying `@[simp]` characterisation lemmas (`hardInterventionOn_J`, `hardInterventionOn_V`, `mem_hardInterventionOn_E`, `mem_hardInterventionOn_L`) let downstream proofs reason about $G_{\doit(W)}$ at the membership level without unfolding the structure.
design_choices:
**No `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition.** The LN writes "let $W \subseteq J \cup V$", but the four component formulas are well-defined for *any* `W : Set α`. A node in `W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` simply gets added to the input set without affecting any edge -- neither `G.E` nor `G.L` touch such a node, by `G.E_subset` and `G.L_subset` -- and `G.V \ W` collapses to `G.V \ (W ∩ G.V)`, the same set the LN's restricted `W` would produce. Dropping the precondition pays off twice: iteration `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁).hardInterventionOn W₂` (claim_3_4) works unconditionally, and every downstream caller is one hypothesis shorter. The trade-off is that, for a `W` not contained in `G.J ∪ G.V`, the spurious nodes show up as inputs of the result; in practice every *claim* that reasons about $G_{\doit(W)}$ adds `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` at the claim level, exactly as the LN does.

**Remove bidirected edges with *either* endpoint in `W`.** The LN's set-builder $\{v \leftrightarrow w \mid v \in G,\, w \in W\}$ reads naturally if bidirected edges are unordered, but Lean encodes them as ordered pairs in `Set (α × α)` plus a symmetry field `L_symm`. A literal port that deleted only pairs with `p.2 ∈ W` would remove `(a, b)` but leave its symmetric partner `(b, a)`, breaking `L_symm` on the result. The chosen encoding `L \ { p | p.1 ∈ W ∨ p.2 ∈ W }` is symmetric under swapping the two components, so `L_symm` lifts directly; this matches the LN's prose "remove all edges into nodes from `W`" under the unordered reading of $\leftrightarrow$.

**Drop the `v ∈ G` clause from the directed-edge deletion.** The LN writes $E \setminus \{v \to w \mid v \in G,\, w \in W\}$, but every directed edge of `G` already has its source in `G.J ∪ G.V = G` by `G.E_subset`, so the clause is redundant when restricted to `G.E`. The cleaner form `G.E \ { p | p.2 ∈ W }` denotes the same set, and the LN's own claim_3_4 proof drops the clause when computing the iterated $E$-component.

**No notation introduced here.** The LN macro `\doit(W)` / subscript $G_{\doit(W)}$ is convenient mathematically, but introducing a Lean `notation` macro at this row -- before any downstream proof exists to motivate the precise token and precedence -- would commit to a choice without a real use case. Callers write `G.hardInterventionOn W` explicitly; a later row can add syntax if the prose form becomes clunky.
